I just want to wish good luck to everyone... | Hi guys. I know that right now it’s probably the most difficult time to find a job as the pandemic has damaged the economy and millions of people have lost their jobs because of that. I don’t think there have been harder times than now, however, it doesn’t mean that all of us should give up. I have seen hundreds, perhaps even thousands of posts saying that people do struggle to find a job and of course this has made me feel quite devastated.

Maybe you lost your job because of covid-19. Maybe you wanted and needed a career change. Maybe you  were sick and tired of your previous job and tried to find something better. Regardless, all of us are in this together. People with outstanding skills and many years of experience don’t feel any better than fresh graduates and those who are about to start their career. Anything’s possible.

I just want you guys to believe in yourselves. Each and every single person is great in their own way, and I’m more than sure that all of you will find a decent job very soon. Don’t give up. Keep trying, keep applying! Watch YouTube videos and/or read articles that would help you succeed as they contain many useful ‘life hacks’ (how to tailor your CV properly, how to write an outstanding and convincing cover letter etc). If you know a few HR Managers, that’s even better, but if you don’t, you can always meet someone either online or in person. I’m sure they would answer all of your questions as well as at least try to help you. 

A good and convincing CV as well as great interview skills will help you get a job even if you’re not a perfect match. Try your best! I know that it’s hard, frustrating and extremely knackering, but still worth it. Try to apply on the companies’ websites if possible and/or call them directly. When you get the job you want you’ll probably realise that all you’d done throughout your job search wasn’t in vain. Be patient because good things come to those who wait.

I’m sure that all of you will be fine soon! Be confident and never give up! Rejections (whether with explanation provided or not) don’t mean that you’re bad or at least not a good candidate, please remember this! Very best of luck!

Not going to lie, the greatest thing about being Experienced is being able to say no to Leetcode questions. | Nothing really else to add.  It just feels pretty good to say that while the job sounds interesting, it's not worth it if I have to interview like that.  Just told two pretty prominent unicorns that I wasn't interesting in the interview process after their recruiters/hiring managers found jobs that I was pretty perfect for.

Funny thing is, I think they hear that a lot from other experienced folks.  There is no surprise on their part and they fully understood.

I'm gainfully employed in a good job with decent pay.  I'm too old and bitter for interview games right now.

EDIT:

I didn't realize that this would gain this much traction.  So, some clarifying points to deal with the questions/assumptions.

My current TC is around $330K - but my refreshers are really good - so it will soon be $370K and $410K the year after.   The company has the ideal culture, great WLB, and I have an absolutely awesome manager.  It's good, but I keep an ear open because I know I can do slightly better.  I have 20 years of experience, 13-15 years as dev (depends on what you call my startup experience), but I have no specialized skills - so no, I won't make $700K that some people are saying that is possible.  Possible for some, not me.  $500K-$550K is my max potential, which is still unbelievably awesome.

The companies I said no to were willing to get up to $500K, but these are non-public companies.  So, there's a risk that lowers the TC in my opinion to something not worth much more than my current job, and my WLB would be much worse.  And I'm not going to cram for LeetCode for a week to work for them.  Too much stress for too much randomness in the interview process.

And no, I don't have a blanket refusal on all LeetCode. I'm not stupid.  But if a company wants me to do LeetCode, I do expect a huge premium.  Most companies don't offer that premium, so I gleefully refuse.

An example of where I didn't refuse - I recently interviewed for the E6 track at Facebook, but failed (long story short, they asked for a 3rd round, got a bad System Design interviewer, and failed - I actually did well on the LeetCode surprisingly).  Anyway, that job would have $500K+ of non-paper money.  So, I will do LeetCode for that.  I also talked with Google.  Did the first round, and they only wanted me at an L5, which is not a huge raise.  That is not worth it to continue, so I refused.

Tomorrow will be two weeks since leaving my toxic work environment. Trying to get my head on straight and focusing on where to go from here, is there anything I'm missing that I should be doing/focusing on? | Tomorrow will officially be two weeks since being free from the toxic work environment I was in, and I have to say, I’ve never felt better! I’ve slept easier and even got up at actual decent times and have the energy to get things done around the house instead of loafing around doing nothing!

The first week I spent for myself, re-adjusting to not working and coming to terms with the environment. I needed breathing room to set my head back on, set myself straight, and I applied for EI. The second week was helping with family members move to their new house; so keeping myself occupied and mind off of being unemployed for the moment. 

I got in contact with my network, had a professional help update and work on my resume and CV, sent out quite a number throughout the two weeks. Hopefully, I will hear about something from one place that I applied for soon enough. Also took the chance to sign up for Microsoft Excel courses, since I want to work on those skills a bit. 

Took the liberty to reach out to different people about courses that I’m looking into making inquiries about different professions that I’ve been looking at maybe changing to a different career while having the time off and giving serious thought on where and what I’d like to do for the rest of my life. I’ve looked at a few avenues that I might want to go down.

What other things do you think I should focus on doing while working towards finding employment again? Skills, industries, etc…?

I hate being a developer | 
Well maybe “hate” is too strong...

I’ve been working for 6 months at a big insurance company as a software developer. I’m doing some web app development by way of pair programming (which I strongly dislike). There isn’t much to complain about, I get paid very well, benefits are great, I never work more than 40 hours a week, people are nice and so on. 

I just don’t like it. I do very little coding, mostly testing, lengthy change request submissions, ci/cd stuff, etc. So the obvious solution would be to find another job, but maybe it’s just software I’m not that into. When I meet other developers, I don’t care to talk about in any form, which is to say I have no real passion or even interest in it. 

I loved being in school and liked my studies well enough, but perhaps it was the goal oriented nature of school I liked. I’ve gotten this far and worked so hard for it, but realizing now I don’t like or find it at all stimulating. This isn’t the life I want. I’m 30 and feeling like I picked the wrong thing because it was objectively a good choice. 

Sorry, I’m just ranting now, but I want to live on a farm and tend to animals or just be connected to my labor in a meaningful way. 

Any insight? Advice?

tl/dr: I worked so hard in school for a job as a software developer and realllllly don’t like it/don’t fit in in corporate world and want to live on a farm.

9 hours programming test | Not so long ago I received a mail from some software company, located in Berlin, Germany. Something like "we're looking for C++ developers of all sorts, both senior and junior, since we have a lot of tasks" and yada-yada. Salary is 80-120k euros per year, relocation to Berlin and all that. C++ is not my main language, but I had some decent experience with it in the past, so I decided to send them a resume, because why not.

Soon after I received an invitation to pass an online programming test, which looked like [this](https://i.imgur.com/9H46lWZ.png).

" *The solution has to be submitted within* ***a 9 hour time frame****. Excellent C++ specialists will of course solve the problem in 3 to 4 hours, and we leave it to the candidates to test their solutions thoroughly before handing them in (in case they finish early).* "

Some of you might be even familiar with this form, because, apparently, these guys have been sending those invitations for YEARS (4 is the least, as far as the google search goes). There were even a couple of threads in this subreddit about them in the past. Most popular responses were *"Guys just want a job done for free with this fake test"* and *"9 hours with NDA not worth it"*. After extensive googling I managed to find the test itself. Some really tricky data structure coding. Despite the NDA agreement, people still post it and ask solutions on stackoverflow (who would have thought, wow). I even found multiple mentions of the same task: first mention was dated 2016 and the last was January 2020. Which kinda proves the point, that the test is legit and they don't get "the work done for free". **It's been EXACTLY the same test FOR YEARS.** 

After a certain amount of thinking, I didn't attempt to take the test anyway. There are a couple of working solutions to it on stackoverflow, so I could just copy and paste them. Or I could come up with my own solution (though, I'm not sure if I'd managed in 9 hours with proper testing and bug fixing). And it's not that I just didn't wanna bother. But instead something in this whole thing felt fishy.

So, yesterday I've got a mail labeled as *"Reminder: Your company-name programming test".* Which contained something like *"we noticed you didn't attempt our test. WE BEG YOU, PLEASE DO. It's hard, but very-very rewarding in the end. Working with us is super-cool, you'll love it."* And it also contained the following sentence: *"In fact, of our now* ***20 full-time developers****, in* ***15 years*** *of company-name,* ***only one*** ***has ever quit*** *their job".*

Now THIS made me freak like hell. I initially assumed that this is just some semi-large startup company with may be 100 engineers and may be 5 people hired yearly. That would explain a very demanding test and the fact, that they've been doing this since 2016 (at least).

But 20 developers??! Only **ONE ever** quit their job in 15 years? What the actual fuck? Are they really looking for **ONE DUDE** for 4 years??! What kind of dude would that be? God walking amongst mere mortals? Dennis Ritchie spiritual reincarnation?! And if the dude they are looking for has to be THAT good - why would he go with them instead of Google?

​

P.S.

Sorry for the long post.

Has any one felt like they are not at 100% operating capacity since the pandemic? | Been feeling a lot of anxiety lately and I want some reassurance that I’m not the only person having slight fuck ups or dropping the ball on things due to the “new normal” of things since the pandemic hit.

Since mid-March we’ve been working from home. I’m in a project management type position at a small publishing company. They let go almost half the company around May and since then I’ve had to pick up the slack in different, unrelated departments and this has caused me to either drop the ball on new projects (that are totally unrelated to my day to day tasks) or have things slip through the cracks in my normal responsibilities. For example, I just realized that 2 weeks ago I forgot to confirm the information on something with an external contact and now it’s possible we’ll have to pay to correct the mistake that I should’ve have double checked on! It’s a relatively small expense and this issue has happened before but it’s never been my fault; this time it’s definitely my fault for not double checking and I am just feeling sick to my stomach. I SHOULD have done X but with the lack of access to higher ups to follow up on minor things and feeling distracted by any number of unrelated tasks throughout the day, it looks like I simply forgot to follow up on that information.

I feel like I’m just making excuses and management is usually understanding but I feel like I’ve had similar slip ups since March. Nothing that has caused the company money but just small mistakes like not formatting something correctly or not sending something on time. I’m still doing and managing so many other things, I’d like to think I’m definitely an asset. But the anxiety of feeling like a fuck up or like I’m gonna get scolded and asked for an explanation other than “I forgot”. Idk, just hoping that others out there are in the same boat of not feeling like they are operating at 100% capacity. Old me was usually so on top of things and I rarely had issues remembering to follow up on things.

I want to quit my job but I have no idea what to do next | So, after 6 years of working as a sheet metal estimator, I want to quit my job.

I’ve been wanting to quit since the beginning of the year and finally get out of New Jersey. I had plans to travel to both San Diego and San Antonio in March to see if they were potential places to move to. But then, coronavirus happened.

Now that the coronavirus pandemic has happened, I now spend mornings at the office and afternoons at home, which would be fine if deadlines to send out bids were more spaced out, suppliers and contractors would actually answer calls/e-mails and if I didn’t have to assume the responsibilities of my immunocompromised parents on top of my job.

Problem is, I have no idea what to do next with my life.

I’m 29, diagnosed with Asperger’s Syndrome, my fine motor skills are poor at best, I lack physical strength, have no social life or network, and am just completely burnt out in general.

I have a bachelor’s degree in broadcast journalism, which as you can tell, has done almost nothing, save for a 9-month stint for a sports news wire service that sold itself and let go nearly the entire staff.

I have no desire to return to college or any form of schooling, as that is where I initially burned out and I don’t need to add more heaps of stress.

Where the hell do I go from here?

Anyone else feeling demotivated in their job search during this time? | TLDR at bottom

For a bit of context, my first job out of college (graduated May 2019) as a web developer only lasted around 6 months. I took a break for one month (for mental health reasons & since Dec is pretty dead in terms of hiring) and started applying at the start of Jan this year. 

For the most part things were going alright, I was getting interviews (which was a pleasant surprise because I thought my short stint would be a major turn off) & in some cases went through several steps that led me to believe that at the bare minimum there was some interest in me. 

Ultimately I was often told that I was a good candidate but of course there was always someone better. So I took this whole process as a numbers game (and believed that with enough applications someone will eventually give me a chance):

- 93 applied 
- 54 ghosted (includes profile views, or status being changed to "under review" but haven't heard back since)
- 25 rejected
- 17 interviews (phone screens, video interviews, in-person interviews, coding tests)
- 9 I withdrew from (some red flags, commute, or turned out to be night shift)
- 5 pending (ghosted in the middle of the process or hiring freeze)
- 1 offer I rejected because of red flags

Fast forward to late Feb-Early March and the pandemic hits hard and suddenly everything is on lockdown. All the companies I was in the middle of interviewing with was hit with a hiring freeze (which is understandable). 

Now I know the IT industry is still pretty intact given that most companies are going virtual and online now, but after going through this rush of balancing phone screens, in-person interviews, coding challenges, & suddenly being met with everything slowing down due to the pandemic (if not stopping altogether), I can't help but feel demotivated.

Lately I can't being myself to continue applying because with everything that's going on, I don't have the mental energy to go on a video interview or answer a coding test that would easily take hours of my time. 

I also can't help but feel like the few applications that I do manage to send end up lost in a void meanwhile the gap on my resume is only going gettingt bigger :(

Anyone else feeling this way? And if so, how are you dealing with it? Thanks!



TLDR: 

Feeling demotivated during the job hunt given the pandemic & was wondering if anyone else can relate & offer some advice. Thanks!

Getting rejected by the same company twice is frustrating | I applied for a job last fall. Went through the motions of applying until the final round, only to be told that I was a very strong candidate but they overestimated the number of spots they had open and didn't have anything for me at the time. They kept sending emails to keep in touch about it from then on until this fall, so for a period of about a year. They hyped me up for the application process this fall and even moved me directly to the final round. Apparently, several management level people had expressed interest in me. I went through the application process again, only to be told that they overestimated the spots they had again, I was a very strong candidate and they want me to apply again next spring or fall. 

What was the point of keeping in contact for a year and having several recruiters from the company reach out every few weeks just to make sure I was still interested in the job? Why would they make an applicant go through the process twice just to reject them with such a reason? 

I've decided I'm done with them. Although application is not hard, there is a mental and emotional component attached to the process and it is exhausting to have to spend months preparing and then anticipating results just to be told that.

I received the news today and I'm probably going to get calls over the next few days. I'm going to be blunt and honest with them.  I don't want to work for a company that is keeping me on the hook as a second choice. If you think I'm good, hire me. If not, stop emailing me with all these claims and getting my hopes up for nothing.

CodeSignal Tips from someone with 844 & 843 | It seems there's some general anguish about CodeSignal so I thought I may share some tips

I have verified scores of [844](https://imgur.com/6bh0luN) and [843](https://imgur.com/zAVdeXL) spaced a year apart and the most recent one was 3 days ago

I lost points on speed exclusively

You'll notice one question is repeated, but I think it's safe to say I don't remember the soln to a q I did a year ago.

​

Tips:

1. Run often, submit often. You don't lose points for submitting many incorrect solns before a correct one, so don't wait. As soon as you have a soln run and submit it.
2. Do one problem at a time. The way the speed score is determined doesn't help you if you choose to jump between problems before solving one since your total time for the problem is aggregated.
3. Don't format your code. After doing a couple of these I'm almost certain that the formatting or cleanliness of your code does not factor into your final score so don't waste time on it like I did.
4. Don't use a verbose language even if it's fast. 70 min isn't a long time, if you're using Java you're shooting yourself in the foot. Use Python or smth similar so you can write up the solns quickly.
5. If CodeSignal won't print your error use a try except (or equivalent) to print your error
6. Practice working with 2D arrays and maps (dicts). Work on iterating through a 2D array in unconventional ways (i.e. column then row, a spiral inwards, a spiral outwards, skipping certain rows, etc) so you get practice.
7. Implement the easy obvious soln first, don't try to optimize until you hit a TLE. Optimized solns can take more time and don't necessarily get you more points.
8. The data structure they give you isn't the data structure you have to work with. Change the data structure of the input variables if it makes your implementation easier/faster. 
9. Prior to taking the CodeSignal practice one topic at a time. Don't jump from DP to Graph Theory to string manipulation. Take the time to do a set of problems in a specific problem space before moving on. That way you truly start recognizing when a problem fits that pattern.

I'd love to hear if anyone disagrees or has any tips to add.

I recognize t's not advantageous for me to share these tips since I'm also competing for the same positions, but I think some great engineers may lose out on positions simply because of the way CodeSignal works and that would suck for everyone.

After 2.5 years and 500+ applications I landed the big one! | Today I signed my offer letter for a role at an upcoming Digital Marketing firm! Since graduating from college in 2018 with a Marketing/Statistics degree I have been trying to land a role in this field with no success.  My first job out of college was with one of the nations largest phone sales companies.  They promised the world to a naive graduate only to crush his workers spirit within 8 months.  Ironically, just having this company on my resume served as a conversation piece for each of the 5 interviews I would land over the next year and a half.  Everyone knew about the sales firm, for mostly notorious reasons.  I spoke highly of them of course so as not to create any red flags for potential employers.  After the wear and tear from pressure sales I went to work as a Taphouse manager.  The mom and pop shop that employed me had me fill several roles.  We opened up a brewery, survived Covid-19, and I leave them today with the biggest(and only) period of growth since their maiden year.  They made a very enticing offer for me to take over the distribution of our beer.  I had every intention of accepting this offer. It was enough for me to accept my lot in life so in July, after the offer was made, I finally stopped applying for a dream job.  On August 28th things changed. The very last business that I had applied to contacted me.  The job sounded like a perfect fit. The pay was more. The hours less. The employer would actually supply health insurance.  Of course, no offer would be made until I interviewed 8 times and completed 2 separate assessments.  After 2 and a half years I finally found a job in my field, and the feeling could not be sweeter.  I’m writing this because of all the times I felt down on my luck.  So many times I would submit application after application feeling like my self worth was at stake.  The constant self doubt.  For all of you job seekers out there don’t give up.  It is hard, depressing, and downright maddening.  That being said, it only takes one win for it to all feel worth it.  I can’t tell you how many of these stories I read during my search, and being able to finally post my own is like a dream come true.  Good luck job seekers!

TLDR: just another finally got a job after what felt like a never ending pile of applications and unfortunate events.
